    luke           2       1  0 20:54 ?        00:00:00 /bin/bash -c ps -ef | rg "pytest tests -q -x|python -m pytest tests -q -x"
    luke           4       2  0 20:54 ?        00:00:00 rg pytest tests -q -x|python -m pytest tests -q -x

• Waited for background terminal · python -m pytest tests -q -x

• Ran python -m pytest tests -q -x
  └ sssssF
    =================================== FAILURES ===================================
    … +670 lines (ctrl + t to view transcript)
    !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! stopping after 1 failures !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
    1 failed, 5 skipped in 15.73s

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

• The direct pytest wrapper is unreliable here after the runner exits, so I’m switching to a simple subprocess capture to get a deterministic return code and failure trace.

• Waited for background terminal · python - <<'PY'
import subprocess, sys
proc = subprocess.run([sys.executable, '-m', 'pytest', 'tests', '-q', '-x'], cwd='.', text=True, capture_output=True)
print('EXIT', proc.returncode)
if proc.stdout:
    print('STDOUT_BEGIN')
    print(proc.stdout)
    print('STDOUT_END')
if proc.stderr:
    print('STDERR_BEGIN')
    print(proc.stderr)
    print('STDERR_END')
PY

• Ran python - <<'PY'
  │ import subprocess, sys
  │ proc = subprocess.run([sys.executable, '-m', 'pytest', 'tests', '-q', '-x'], cwd='.', text=True, capture_output=True)
  │ … +10 lines
  └ EXIT 1
    STDOUT_BEGIN
    … +674 lines (ctrl + t to view transcript)

    STDOUT_END

• Working (8m 40s • esc to interrupt) · 1 background terminal running · /ps to view · /stop to close


› Find and fix a bug in @filename

  gpt-5.4 high · ~/Documents/code/murder
